The UK government must urgently issue a formal apology for the state’s role in forced adoption as many victims are nearing the end of their lives, a cross-party group of MPs has said.

A report from the education select committee said ministers should provide an initial commitment to an apology and begin working with survivor groups as quickly as possible on its wording.

Значительная часть средств (около трети) будет выделена на поддержание и обновление дорожной сети, включая ямочный ремонт и реновацию.

The government said it was aiming to “fix the foundations” with almost a third, £8.4bn, of the spending going on maintenance, including resurfacing a quarter of England’s strategic road network.

articles.title: Fossil fuel companies finally accept the climate crisis – just not their role in it
articles.summary: Крупнейшие компании, занимающиеся добычей и переработкой ископаемого топлива, признают реальность и антропогенную природу изменения климата в судебных разбирательствах по всему миру.  При этом, несмотря на это признание, они не признают свою ответственность за развитие климатического кризиса.  Таким образом, происходит смена стратегии от отрицания к признанию проблемы в юридической сфере.
articles.original_description:
The era of corporate climate denial is over but in courts around the world the big names have shifted strategy

articles.title: More frequent ejaculations may boost men’s fertility, research suggests
articles.summary:
Need for abstinence before fertility treatment questioned as study finds sperm deteriorates as it stays in body

Encouraging men to have more frequent ejaculations may boost their fertility, according to researchers who found that sperm deteriorates over time as it remains in the body.

The longer men went without sex, the more their sperm showed signs of DNA damage and oxidative stress, and the more tests rated the sperm as less viable and poorer swimmers.

articles.title: This cow uses tools like a primate—and scientists are stunned
articles.summary: A cow named Veronika has stunned scientists by using tools in a flexible and purposeful way. She chooses different ends of a brush depending on the part of her body and adjusts her movements accordingly. This level of tool use is incredibly rare and was previously seen mainly in primates. The finding hints that cows may be much smarter than we assume.

articles.title: Vivid dreams may be the secret to deeper, more restful sleep
articles.summary: Vivid dreams might be doing more than just entertaining your mind at night. Researchers found that immersive dreaming can actually make sleep feel deeper and more refreshing, even when brain activity is high. Surprisingly, people reported their deepest sleep after intense dream experiences, not just during quiet, inactive periods. This suggests dreams may play a key role in helping us feel truly rested.

articles.title: Scientists found a bug that generates its own heat in freezing cold
articles.summary: Snow flies have an unexpected way of surviving freezing temperatures. They produce antifreeze proteins to block ice formation and can even generate their own heat. Scientists also found that their genes are unusually unique, and they feel less cold-related pain than other insects. These combined traits let them stay active in conditions that would freeze most species.

articles.title: Deepfake X-rays are so real even doctors can’t tell the difference
articles.summary: Deepfake X-rays created by AI are now convincing enough to fool both doctors and AI models. In tests, radiologists had limited success identifying fake images, especially when they didn’t know they were being shown. This opens the door to risks like fraudulent medical claims and tampered diagnoses. Experts say stronger safeguards and detection tools are critical as the technology advances.

articles.title: Metformin’s hidden brain pathway revealed after 60 years
articles.summary: Новое исследование показало, что метформин влияет не только на организм, но и на мозг. Препарат снижает уровень сахара в крови, воздействуя через ранее неизвестный путь, связанный с переключением ключевого белка и активацией определенных нейронов. Это открытие может стать основой для разработки новых методов лечения диабета.
articles.original_description: A major discovery reveals that metformin works not just in the body, but in the brain. By switching off a key protein and activating specific neurons, the drug lowers blood sugar through a previously hidden pathway, opening new doors for diabetes treatment.

articles.title: Astronomers solve 50-year mystery of a naked-eye star’s extreme X-rays
articles.summary: Астрономы разрешили 50-летнюю загадку необычно мощных рентгеновских лучей звезды, видимой невооруженным глазом. Благодаря точным наблюдениям космического телескопа XRISM, было обнаружено, что источник излучения – скрытый белый карлик, затягивающий вещество и генерирующий экстремальную тепловую энергию. Это открытие не только объясняет природу звезды Кассиопеи, но и подтверждает существование предсказанного типа двойных звездных систем.
articles.original_description: A star you can see with the naked eye has kept astronomers guessing for decades with its unusually powerful X-rays. Now, thanks to highly precise observations from Japan’s XRISM space telescope, scientists have finally uncovered the source: a hidden white dwarf companion pulling in material and generating extreme heat. This discovery not only solves a 50-year-old mystery surrounding Gamma Cassiopeiae, but also confirms the existence of a long-predicted type of binary star system.

articles.title: Project Hail Mary meets reality: 45 planets could harbor alien life
articles.summary: Astronomers have narrowed down the cosmic search for life, identifying fewer than 50 rocky planets among thousands of known exoplanets that may have the right conditions to support life. Using new data from ESA’s Gaia mission and NASA archives, researchers pinpointed worlds in the “habitable zone,” where temperatures could allow liquid water to exist. Some of the most intriguing targets include nearby systems like TRAPPIST-1 and Proxima Centauri, offering tantalizing possibilities just dozens of

articles.title: Scientists open a million-year-old time capsule beneath New Zealand
articles.summary: Ученые в Новой Зеландии обнаружили в пещере капсулу времени возрастом миллион лет, содержащую останки 16 видов животных, включая предка какапо, возможно, умевшего летать. Палеонтологические находки свидетельствуют о частых вулканических извержениях и резких изменениях климата, которые формировали экосистемы Новой Зеландии задолго до появления человека, предоставляя уникальную возможность изучить утраченный период в истории природы.
articles.original_description: Deep inside a cave, scientists uncovered fossils from 16 species, including a newfound kākāpō ancestor that may have been able to fly. These remains reveal that New Zealand’s ecosystems were constantly disrupted by volcanic eruptions and rapid climate shifts. Long before humans, waves of extinction and replacement reshaped the islands’ wildlife. It’s a rare window into a missing chapter of natural history.

articles.title: Scientists find immune cell linked to long COVID fatigue and symptoms
articles.summary: Long COVID remains a frustrating medical mystery, affecting up to 1 in 10 people long after the initial infection fades. Now, scientists have uncovered a crucial clue hidden deep within the immune system. By analyzing individual immune cells, they identified a distinct molecular state in key white blood cells—especially common in patients who initially had mild or moderate COVID.

articles.title: Scientists discover “overflow valve” in cells linked to Parkinson’s Disease
articles.summary: Researchers have identified a crucial ion channel, TMEM175, that acts like an overflow valve in the cell’s recycling system. It regulates acidity inside lysosomes, ensuring they function properly. When TMEM175 is faulty, toxic buildup can occur, contributing to diseases like Parkinson’s. This breakthrough reveals a promising new target for future therapies.

articles.title: Scientists discover why this deadly lung cancer keeps coming back
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered that losing a key protein in small cell lung cancer triggers inflammation that actually helps tumors grow and spread. Even more surprising, it pushes cancer cells into a more aggressive, neuron-like state linked to relapse.
articles.original_description: Scientists have discovered that losing a key protein in small cell lung cancer triggers inflammation that actually helps tumors grow and spread. Even more surprising, it pushes cancer cells into a more aggressive, neuron-like state linked to relapse.

articles.title: Most mass spectrometers handle a few molecules. This new prototype handles billions
articles.summary: Новый прототип масс-спектрометра способен анализировать миллиарды молекул одновременно, что значительно превосходит возможности существующих приборов. Это достижение повышает чувствительность и открывает перспективы для обнаружения редких молекул, что может революционизировать такие области, как разработка лекарств и исследования в области одиночных клеток.
articles.original_description: A new prototype could supercharge mass spectrometry by analyzing thousands of molecules at once instead of one by one. The breakthrough boosts sensitivity enough to reveal rare, hidden molecules—potentially transforming fields like drug discovery and single-cell biology.

articles.title: What you do in midlife could reveal how long you’ll live
articles.summary: By closely monitoring fish throughout their lives, researchers found that simple behaviors in midlife—like movement and sleep—can predict lifespan. Fish that stayed active and slept mostly at night tended to live longer, while those slowing down earlier lived shorter lives. Surprisingly, aging didn’t unfold smoothly but in sudden jumps between stages. The work suggests that tracking daily habits in humans could reveal early clues about how we age.

articles.title: 24 new deep-sea species found including a rare new branch of life
articles.summary: In a remarkable deep-sea breakthrough, researchers have discovered 24 new species of amphipods in the Pacific’s Clarion-Clipperton Zone—including a rare, entirely new superfamily. The findings reveal previously unknown branches of life and push the boundaries of how deep these creatures are known to live.

articles.title: Cold weather linked to 40,000 extra heart deaths each year in the U.S.
articles.summary: When temperatures plunge, the risk to your heart rises dramatically. A large U.S. study shows cold weather is linked to far more cardiovascular deaths than heat, accounting for tens of thousands of extra deaths each year. Scientists found the safest temperature sits around 74°F, with danger increasing as conditions get colder—or hotter. As more people live with chronic illnesses, the threat from extreme cold may only intensify.

articles.title: Scientists just discovered bees and hummingbirds are drinking alcohol
articles.summary: Flower nectar often contains small amounts of alcohol, meaning pollinators like hummingbirds are drinking it all day long. Despite consuming human-equivalent amounts, they show no signs of intoxication—suggesting a surprising evolutionary tolerance.

articles.title: Honey bees dance better with an audience
articles.summary: Honey bees don’t just perform their famous waggle dance to share directions, they actually adjust how well they dance depending on who’s watching. Researchers found that when fewer bees pay attention, the dancer becomes less precise as it moves around trying to attract an audience. This means the dance is not simply a fixed message about food location, but a flexible performance shaped by social feedback.

articles.title: Why your brain may be sabotaging your balance as you age
articles.summary: Balance problems in aging and Parkinson’s may come from the body working too hard, not too little. Scientists found that the brain and muscles become overactive during even minor disturbances, yet this actually weakens balance recovery. At the same time, muscles can stiffen against each other, making movement less stable. This unexpected pattern could help predict who is more likely to fall.

articles.title: Fathers face rising depression risk a year after baby arrives
articles.summary: New fathers appear to have fewer mental health diagnoses during pregnancy and the early months after birth. But that early stability does not last. About a year later, depression and stress-related disorders increase significantly, surprising researchers. The findings suggest that the emotional toll of fatherhood builds over time rather than hitting immediately.

articles.original_description: Researchers have identified microRNA-93 as a key genetic driver of fatty liver disease and discovered that vitamin B3 can effectively shut it down. This finding suggests a safe, widely available vitamin could become a powerful new treatment.

articles.title: EPA approves sale of higher-ethanol fuel in bid to lower gas prices
articles.summary:
Higher blend has been prohibited in warm weather because of concerns it could worsen smog

The US Environmental Protection Agency said on Wednesday that it would temporarily allow widespread sales of a higher-ethanol gas blend in a move that it hopes will tamp down consumer prices that have soared since the Iran war began.

The higher-ethanol blend has been prohibited in warm weather because of concerns it could worsen smog.

articles.title: Rise of the shrubs: what happened when scientists heated a Rocky Mountain wildlife meadow by 2C?
articles.summary:
A long-running experiment in Colorado provides an ‘alarming’ view of how rapidly unchecked global heating could transform fragile ecosystems

Every summer, people descend on the wildflower capital of Colorado to see grasslands flush with corn lilies, aspen sunflowers and sub-alpine larkspur. In January 1991, scientists set up a unique experiment in these Rocky Mountain meadows. It was one of the first (and longest running) to work out how the changing climate would affect an ecosystem.</
